text: The surgeon performs a robotic right radical nephrectomy and IVC tumor thrombectomy on a patient positioned in a right side up modified flank position. A 12mm assistant umbilical port and four robotic ports are placed along the midclavicular paramedian lines. The inferior vena cava is identified after mobilizing the ascending colon and cocorizing the duodenum. A laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound probe surveys the IVC, revealing thrombus in the suprarenal IVC. Bulldog vascular clamps are applied to the contralateral renal vein and IVC. A cavotomy is made on the anterior surface of the IVC, exposing tumor invasion into the IVC wall and posterior lumbar branches. A 5.5 cm segment of the IVC is resected, and a 6 cm tubularized synthetic polyester graft is anastomosed to the native IVC using PTFE suture. The infrarenal bulldog clamp is flashed open to flush out air and clots before completing the anastomosis. The clamps are removed, and intraoperative ultrasound confirms graft patency.

In the second case, the surgeon inspects the inferior vena cava (IVC) where an extruded IVC filter has eroded through the wall. After therapeutic heparinization, the IVC is cross-clamped and a longitudinal cavotomy is made along its anterior surface. The embedded filter is removed piece by piece, followed by resection of the desmoplastic reaction and calcifications from the IVC wall. An eight-centimeter synthetic patch graft is anastomosed to the defect using Five-O PTFE suture, ensuring a watertight closure.
